APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The paper addresses an NLP/ML-relevant problem (automatic detection of MCI from speech transcripts) and uses standard NLP/ML tools (word embeddings, complex networks, Bojanowski et al. 2016 embeddings, SVM/RF, ensemble and multi-view learning) evaluated on multiple benchmark datasets (DementiaBank, Cinderella, ABCD) — see Sections 4.1 and 4.3. The evaluation compares to appropriate baselines (Coh‑Metrix linguistic features, BoW) and reports comparable accuracies, which matches community expectations for empirical NLP/clinical‑ML work. It is therefore well targeted to a top NLP venue (or an application/clinical track of a top ML venue), though it is primarily an applied/empirical contribution with limited ML methodological novelty, which is the main reason I rate fit as very good rather than perfect.

IMPACT.justification: The paper tackles the important clinical problem of automatic MCI detection from speech, emphasizing early diagnosis and fully automated, language-independent assessment. Its proposed method—enriching word-adjacency complex networks with word embeddings to handle short, disfluent transcripts—demonstrates practical gains (outperforming linguistic features across three datasets, with ensemble/multi-view improvements such as +10% on Cinderella and 80% accuracy on Arizona/ABCD). This approach is readily adoptable by researchers and clinicians working on multilingual/low-resource clinical NLP and opens new directions in using topology+embeddings for short-text clinical classification, so it is likely to have strong, though not transformative, impact.

CLARITY.justification: The paper has a logical section structure (datasets → methods → experiments) but presentation is marred by extensive repetition, duplicated paragraphs (e.g., multiple repeated blocks about the word adjacency model and prior work) and formatting artifacts that disrupt flow. Key contributions are only summarized late in the text rather than clearly stated up front, and many typographical/grammatical errors and misspellings are present (e.g., "trainning", "vertice", "syntatic") which reduce readability. Figures and tables are referenced (Table 1/3, Figure 3) but not shown here and several citations/lines appear garbled, indicating the need for careful copyediting and reorganization for clarity.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares its proposed complex-network (CN+embeddings) features to reasonable baselines (Bag-of-Words and linguistic features from Coh-Metrix) and reports results on common datasets (DementiaBank, Cinderella, ABCD), noting that accuracies are comparable to values reported in prior work (e.g., “accuracies found here are comparable to... 60% to 85%”). It also cites and discusses many relevant prior studies (Roark et al., Fraser et al., Davy et al., Vincze et al., etc.). However, the text does not fully document whether experimental conditions and splits match prior work (so fairness is unclear) and it lacks direct head-to-head comparisons with some recent or stronger model classes (e.g., end-to-end embedding/NN baselines), and the analysis of why CN outperforms baselines is limited.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper states clear claims (e.g. “complex networks are suitable to detect MCI and outperform linguistic features in all datasets” and reports improvements such as 80% accuracy on the Arizona dataset and a 10% gain on Cinderella), and it describes datasets, feature types and classifiers (Sections 4.1–4.3). However, essential experimental details are missing in the provided text: no description of evaluation protocol (cross‑validation or train/test splits), no handling of class imbalance, no hyperparameter selection or baselines’ numeric results, and no statistical significance testing; the network enrichment procedure (thresholding/weighting of cosine similarities) is also underspecified. Mathematical content is limited to standard graph measures and is correct as stated, but the empirical claims lack sufficient experimental controls and reporting to be fully supported.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The experiments cover multiple datasets (DementiaBank, Cinderella, ABCD) and compare complex-network (CN) topology, BoW and linguistic features across several classifiers and ensemble/multi-view setups (Sections 4.1.1, 4.2.1, 4.3), with reported improvements (e.g. 10% on Cinderella, 80% on Arizona). However, there is no explicit ablation or sensitivity analysis of the key design choices (e.g., how embeddings are used to add edges or any cosine-similarity thresholds for enrichment, and no variation of embedding hyperparameters beyond a single default setting), nor are statistical significance tests or detailed failure/limitation analyses presented. Reproducibility is partial: some preprocessing and embedding params are stated (filled-pause removal, DeepBond, Bojanowski et al. defaults) but crucial implementation/hyperparameter details, training/validation splits and enrichment thresholds are missing. The related work coverage is thorough, but overall methodological analysis lacks the ablations and negative-result discussion needed for stronger support of the claims.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper primarily combines established techniques — word-adjacency (co-occurrence) complex networks and standard topological measures — with existing embedding-based network enrichment (citing Perozzi et al., 2014) and subword embeddings (Bojanowski et al., 2016) (see Sec. 4.2.1 and embedding description). The main novelty is an application/adaptation of these methods to short, noisy clinical speech transcripts for MCI detection (including use of subword embeddings to handle OOV and adapting enrichment for short texts), plus ensemble/multi-view fusion, which is a modest but useful extension rather than a fundamentally new method. Overall this is an incremental contribution that would be informative but not surprising to experts in NLP/clinical ML.
